title: AR: Predicting from Past Values
concept: Autoregression (AR) forecasts future values in a time series by running regression on its own previous values (known as lags).

exercises.prompt: Recap: In time series forecasting, what does a 'lag' represent?

options.text: A past value of the variable, such as yesterday's value used to predict today's
A delay in loading the machine learning model's output in the app
The time it takes to collect data from a physical sensor

exercises.explain: Perfect! A lag represents a past step in time. If we predict today's sales using yesterday's sales, yesterday's sales are a lag of 1 day (Lag 1).

cards.title: Predicting Tomorrow's Weather
cards.body: If you want to guess tomorrow's temperature, the most important clue is usually today's temperature. If it's 80°F today, it will likely be close to 80°F tomorrow. Instead of looking at external factors, we can predict the future simply by looking at our own past.

cards.title: What is Autoregression (AR)?
cards.body: Autoregression (AR) is a forecasting method where the model predicts the next value in a sequence by running a regression on its own previous values. The prefix 'auto-' means 'self' — so autoregression is literally regressing a variable on itself.

cards.title: Driving by the Rearview Mirror
cards.body: Imagine steering a car on a long, straight road by only looking through your rearview mirror. By seeing where you were 1 second ago (Lag 1) and 2 seconds ago (Lag 2), you can predict where you should steer next. You assume the path you've been on will continue.

cards.title: Ice Cream Sales Lags
cards.body: To forecast today's ice cream shop sales, an AR model might look back at the past two days. If yesterday's sales were $500 (Lag 1) and the day before was $450 (Lag 2), the model assigns weights to these lags to estimate today's sales.

cards.title: Not All Lags are Equal
cards.body: You might think an AR model gives the same importance to every day in the past. In reality, more recent days (like yesterday) usually have a much stronger influence on the prediction than days further back (like two weeks ago).

cards.title: Standard Regression vs. Autoregression
cards.body: How standard regression and autoregression differ in their input variables:

left.text:
Predicts using different variables.
e.g. Predicts crop yield using rainfall and fertilizer amount.

right.text:
Predicts using past values of the same variable.
e.g. Predicts today's crop price using yesterday's crop price.

exercises.prompt: What does the 'auto' in Autoregression mean?

options.text: Self — because the model regresses the variable on its own past values
Automatic — because the model requires no manual training
Automobile — because it was invented to predict traffic flows

exercises.explain: Correct! 'Auto' means self. Autoregression predicts a variable's future by looking at its own past.

exercises.prompt: True or False: An Autoregression model uses external features like humidity and wind speed to predict tomorrow's rainfall.
exercises.explain: False. Autoregression models rely purely on past values of the target variable itself (past rainfall), not external variables.

exercises.sentence: An AR model that looks back at the past two steps in time is using two ___.
exercises.explain: Lags represent previous points in time (such as Lag 1 and Lag 2) used to make the prediction.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following describes an Autoregression (AR) prediction process?

options.text: Predicting today's stock price using yesterday's and the day before yesterday's stock price
Predicting stock price using news article sentiment and interest rates
Grouping stocks into technology, retail, and energy sectors

exercises.explain: Yes! Relying on past values of the same variable to forecast the future is autoregression.

exercises.prompt: You are setting up an Autoregression (AR) model to forecast daily website traffic. You decide to look back 3 days (Lag 1, Lag 2, and Lag 3) to predict tomorrow's traffic. What is this lookback window called in time series terminology, and how does the model use it?

options.text: It is called the number of lags. The model treats these 3 past days as independent inputs in a regression to calculate the next day's traffic.
It is called the error correction window. The model averages the mistakes of the past 3 days and adds them to today's traffic.
It is called differencing. The model subtracts the traffic of Day 3 from Day 1 to find the growth trend.
It is called seasonal adjustments. The model compares this week's traffic to last year's traffic.

exercises.explain: Perfect! Autoregression uses past observations (lags) as the input features for a regression model to predict the next value. Now, what happens when there's a sudden, unexpected shock? Let's check out Moving Average (MA) next!
